future.title: Future outlook and company philosophy · Debatable
future.script: This page lays out the company's long-term thinking. The core bet is that as writing gets easier to generate with AI, spoken argument under pressure becomes a more honest test of whether someone actually understands what they're talking about. You can produce a polished essay from either side of most questions now. What's harder to fake is standing up, taking interruptions, and defending your claim while someone questions it live. The page walks through why that shift matters for schools, employers, and students; what the product does about it; and where the platform goes next. Right now you can practice rounds against AI opponents, spar with real people, and earn a Communication Score that comes with a public verify link. Next up are program tools for schools, cross-language debate rooms, and eventually watchable rounds with audience judging drills. The format list spans APDA, BP, Policy, LD, Worlds, and several others, all treated as their own rooms. The philosophy is not that writing dies; it's that live speech becomes the second layer of proof when writing alone stops being enough.

pricing.title: Debatable Pricing · Individuals, teams, schools, and business prep
pricing.script: Right now, every tier is completely free while the platform is in beta. The prices shown here are what you will pay once beta ends, so you can plan ahead. The free tier gives you ten signed-in requests to try a real round with standard depth and six debate brains. The individual plan is ten dollars per year and gets you two hundred fifty requests per month, all six brains, HD voice, pro model tiers, competition depth, full case tooling, and memory that adapts every session. It also includes ten seats if you want to invite a small prep pod. The team plan is fifty dollars per year for fifty seats and fifteen hundred pooled monthly requests, plus an admin dashboard and shared paradigm bank. If you are running a bigger program, multiple teams, or need invoicing and privacy paperwork, email about institutional access. The pricing is annual by design, closer to a tournament fee than a monthly software bill. If you cancel, you keep full access until your billing period ends, then drop back to the free tier.

landing.title: Debatable · Debate Online, Judged Live
landing.script: This is the home page for Debatable, a place where you can debate out loud and get an instant written verdict. You can argue against an AI opponent that interrupts and pushes back, or match with a real person online. Either way, when the clock runs out, an AI judge writes a full ballot: who won, why, and speaker points for every speech. Fifteen debate formats are supported, from Public Forum and British Parliamentary to Policy and World Schools, and you can debate in fourteen languages. The first round runs without signing in; after that, sign in with Google to save your ballots and track your progress across ten rounds. Every round you speak counts toward a cross-format leaderboard. The AI judge does not invent evidence or fabricate tournament history; it scores based on persuasion, clash, and structure. Some rounds are scheduled and streamed live so anyone can watch. A part of the platform is The Floor, a prediction market where you stake play credits on who will win a given round, and your Sharp Score tracks how early you called it. Right now everything is free while the platform is in beta. If you want to bring this to your school, there is a paid ambassador program. Start by choosing whether you want to debate the AI, find a live opponent, or watch a round already running.

why-debateit.title: Why Debatable Is Different | Debatable
why-debateit.script: This page explains why Debatable is built differently from a general chatbot. Most language models are helpful assistants that answer whatever you ask. Debatable is a training partner. It runs timed speeches, argues the opposing side, tracks the clash between positions, writes judge ballots, and remembers what you worked on across rounds. The comparison chart shows the contrast. A typical chat tool adds voice on top of text and stays agreeable unless you fight with your own prompts. Debatable starts voice-first, interrupts like a real timer, takes the other side automatically, enforces debate formats without you explaining the rules every time, and delivers feedback as a ballot with speaker points and recurring patterns. Its memory is debate-specific, not general conversation history. Your rounds feed a coaching layer that builds a knowledge vault and suggests the next drill. The workflow is simple. You speak and take a position under time. The opponent clashes with your reasoning. You read the ballot to see what won and what needs work. That history becomes the foundation for future rounds. You can start a voice round immediately or build your debate brain by uploading background first.

story.title: Why this exists · Debatable
story.script: This page explains why Debatable was built and who made it. The founder is a national APDA champion who wanted voice-first AI that could hold a position, push back on arguments, and write a real judge ballot after the round. That opponent didn't exist, so she wrote it. The bottleneck in debate practice has always been finding someone to take rounds with you when your teammates are tired or unavailable. Debatable solves that by giving you as many reps as you want, any format, any hour. It doesn't replace a coach; coaches build judgment and name the habits you can't see in yourself. But coaching is scarce and practice shouldn't be. The page also lays out what's under the hood: nine debate formats tuned separately, six AI brains routed per task, sixteen distinct voices. The team is four people, all debaters, no investors. Below that you'll find a timeline showing how the system evolved and a list of open problems the builder would take help on, like realistic interruption handling and multilingual format-specific register. From here you can take a voice round or find a human partner.

learn.title: Learn AI Debate · APDA, BP, PF, LD Format Guides · Debatable
learn.script: This is the learning hub where you find everything written down before you argue it out loud. The fundamentals walk you through six core moves: how to build an argument with claim, warrant, and impact; how judges weigh magnitude against probability; how to rebut with links, turns, and mitigations; how to signpost so the judge can flow your structure in real time; how to use cross-ex to plant concessions instead of picking fights; and how to sound like varsity instead of novice. Each lesson is a two-minute read. Once you've read all six, run the tutorial to practice them against the AI. Below that you'll find format guides for APDA, BP, PF, LD, Policy, and World Schools, covering speech order, time limits, and what judges actually reward in each format. The education section gives you subject primers on topics like inflation, international relations, and climate policy, each with a five-question quiz so you can test what stuck before you argue a motion on that topic. The glossary has sixty-five debate terms defined in one place. Start with the fundamentals if this is your first time. Come back to the format guides when you need the exact rules for a specific circuit.

schools.title: Debatable for Schools · Voice-first debate practice
schools.script: This page is for teachers, coaches, and school administrators who want to add debate training to their program. The problem most schools face is not whether debate works, but whether they can run it. You need judges, practice partners, and a travel budget. Lose any one and the program stops. Debatable handles the practice volume so your coach can focus on the students who need face time most. Here is how it works. The coach assigns a motion in whatever format your team runs: APDA, Policy, Lincoln-Douglas, Public Forum, Congressional, or Worlds. The student argues out loud against an AI opponent that knows the format. At the end, they get a real ballot with speaker points, a decision paragraph, their best line, what they dropped, and one drill to practice next. The coach can review every transcript. It is a practice partner, not a curriculum replacement. The school plan is a flat per-school license covering every student. Students can also try it for free on their own. Every round over a certain threshold earns a verifiable credential. If you are evaluating this for a district, the privacy section at the bottom answers the data, age-gate, and procurement questions your legal team will ask.

professionals.title: Professionals and use cases · Debatable
professionals.script: This page lays out how Debatable works outside competitive debate. It's the same core tool, a live AI opponent that argues back and a judge that scores you afterward, but applied to jobs where you defend something out loud and have to hold it under questioning. Pre-law students use it to rehearse moot court cross-examination and Socratic cold calls. Consultants run case interviews against an AI that pushes on their assumptions and forces them to defend recommendations the way a partner would. People preparing for town halls, investor Q&A, dissertation defenses, or board meetings practice the hardest part: the follow-up questions, the interruptions, the moment someone leans on the weakest part of your answer. Each section links to a version of the tool configured for that context. The engine is the same one that drills competitive debaters; it just retools for any setting where someone sharp is going to question your reasoning in real time and you need to hold your position instead of retreating.

coaches.title: Coaches · Debate coach directory · Debatable
coaches.script: This is a community directory of debate coaches. Anyone can browse coaches across every circuit, school, college, and independent programs, all listed in one place. You can search for a coach and reach out to them directly. If you're a coach yourself, you can list your own information so debaters can find you. Adding a coach takes about a minute and puts your contact details where people actually look. There's no middleman; debaters reach you however you specify, by email, phone, or link. Browsing is open to everyone. Adding a coach requires a quick Google sign-in. The directory is built by the community, so what you see depends on who has added themselves. If you're looking for coaching or offering it, this is where the circuit gathers that information in a single searchable place.

community.title: Community · Debatable
community.script: This is where published cases, replies, and full rounds live. You can read what other people argued, then argue back. Right now, about a hundred people opened the app in the last month, so the feed is still filling in. Some threads are seeded by AI personas to get things moving; those are always labeled. You can publish a case after you finish a round on the debate-it page, save it, and hit publish from your team case library or the post-round screen. You can also start a new thread in discussions, ask a question, post a take, or fight about a motion. Each published round gets a permanent URL with the motion as the title, the full transcript, and a button to try the motion yourself. There is also a Discord where you can propose a time, recruit judges, and get a same-day ballot. The founder funds this personally and is raising money to build it properly; the best way to help is to use it and report what breaks.

credentials.title: Debatable Communication Credential · Prove you can think on your feet
credentials.script: Anything you can prepare in advance, a model can help you produce. It cannot sit in the chair for you when someone pushes back in real time. This page explains a credential that measures exactly that: your ability to make a complex case out loud, under interruption, with no second draft. You argue for about eight minutes against something that argues back. A judge scores five dimensions of how you handled it, clarity, reasoning, responsiveness, listening, persuasion, and issues a score out of 100, a worldwide percentile, and a one-page certificate with a verify URL anyone can check in two clicks. Clear the median and the credential is shareable at one of four levels: Effective, Advanced, Elite, or Exceptional. The assessment is a structured live exam with fixed timing, identical for everyone. You pick a motion and a side, argue out loud, answer pushback, weigh competing fronts, and summarize the opponent's case before responding. The certificate lives at a public verify page. Every credential carries your Communication Score, your percentile, a five-dimension radar, and round history. Open it and the whole picture is on one screen.

research.title: Research corpus · Debatable
research.script: Debatable collects every round people run and turns it into a corpus: the motion, both sides, the judge's ballot, all timestamped. That library trains the AI overnight. Research groups can license the slice that users chose to share, after stripping names and account data. The page shows what's inside: voice transcripts with no audio stored, rounds from nine formats including APDA and Policy, dozens of languages. You see sample rows, a rebuttal exchange, a judge ballot with speaker points, a Policy contention with tagged evidence. Each record pairs the argument with the outcome, so labs get real clash under time pressure plus the verdict, not just monologue text scraped from blogs. The opt-in toggle lives in every profile, off by default; flip it and future rounds carry a shareable flag. The counts are early, the system went live last month, but the nightly loop has been writing since May. The wedge is what the dataset becomes at scale when thousands of people argue daily. Labs interested in a sample export can reach out through the email linked at the bottom. The consent language is in privacy section six.

reviews.title: Reviews · Debatable
reviews.script: This is where circuit debaters explain why they use Debatable. You'll hear from national champions and world-stage competitors across four formats: World Schools, Public Forum, British Parliamentary, and American Parliamentary. They all say the same thing in different words: the real bottleneck in debate isn't talent, it's access to serious practice. One captained Zimbabwe at Worlds and competed at Columbia. Another took first at NSDA Nationals in Public Forum. A third reached octos at the World Universities Debating Championship. They talk about the AI arguing back at full strength, ballots that show exactly where a round was won or lost, and a platform that sharpens thinking instead of replacing it. One compares it to the chess dot com of debate. These aren't testimonials written for marketing; they're explanations from people who've competed at the highest level and know what makes practice valuable. If you want to know whether this tool is built for your format, these reviews answer that question.

us.title: Debatable for the US · PF, LD, Policy, Worlds, Congress
us.script: This page is for debaters on the US circuit. You can practice Public Forum, Lincoln-Douglas, Policy, World Schools, Big Questions, or Congressional Debate with an AI opponent that runs each format the way your actual tournaments do. Real speech times, proper structure, motions pulled from TOC, Glenbrooks, Harvard. The AI judges to paradigm: it'll check for extension in PF summary, hold you to framework in LD, and follow tagged evidence in Policy. When you pick a side and argue out loud, you get a ballot with speaker points and a reason for decision in seconds. The motion banks match what you'd see at major invitationals; the AI argues from US policy fights and cases your judge already knows, not generic claims. If you're new to a format, the guide pages walk you through structure and expectations. If you already compete, this is drills between tournaments. Choose your format, start a round, and the AI takes the other bench.

india.title: Debatable for India · Asian Parli, BP, WSDC, MUN Practice
india.script: This page is the Indian circuit landing zone. It gives you an AI debate partner that runs Asian Parliamentary, British Parliamentary, World Schools, and MUN the way tournaments in Delhi, Bangalore, Mumbai, and Kolkata actually run them. You argue out loud or in typed text, and the AI plays the rest of the bench while a judge writes your ballot. The motion banks pull from IIDC, UADC, NSDC, IRC, AIDC, the IIT-Delhi Open, NLSIU Bangalore, Frank Anthony Memorial, and CBSE inter-school. The AI argues from Indian and South Asian cases: CAA, reservation, electoral bonds, demonetisation, the 2024 election, Bangladesh 2024, Sri Lanka bankruptcy. It holds roles the way an Indian adjudicator expects. Seven-minute substantives for Asian Parli, four teams for BP, eight-minute speeches for WSDC, position-paper style for MUN. The voice round runs full Hindi rounds, model and transcription both. You can also practice university vivas, IIT and NEET interviews, and board practicals with an Indian-English examiner voice. Every account gets five rounds anonymously and ten once you sign in. The product is in beta so every tier costs zero right now. Click start a round to jump in, or pick a format guide to read the rules first.

uwc.title: Debatable for UWCs · Free Pro access for every UWC
uwc.script: This page is for United World Colleges specifically. If your school is one of the eighteen UWCs, you can claim a design partner slot and get free Pro access for your entire campus, this year and the next, with no strings attached. The builder is a UWC-USA alumnus who competed nationally and wants every UWC in the first cohort before opening it wider. Here's what that actually means. Your debate or MUN advisor books one thirty-minute call where the founder walks them through the platform. Students then run practice rounds against an AI opponent in any of fifteen formats, including Worlds, British Parliamentary, Model UN, Asian Parli, Public Forum, Lincoln-Douglas, Policy, and Congress. Every round ends with a judge ballot that includes speaker points, key clashes, and drill recommendations. The AI runs in fourteen languages with localized accent, so multilingual cohorts can test the voice feature across Hindi, Mandarin, Spanish, French, Arabic, German, and English in the same week. The ask in return is simple. One onboarding call, one mid-year feedback note, and optional permission to credit your school in a design partner list. No contract, no procurement cycle, no exclusivity. If it doesn't fit after two years, you keep using the free tier and part on good terms. Schedule the call to claim your slot.

high-school.title: Debatable: High School Edition · AI debate prep
high-school.script: Run a full practice round in any high school format: Public Forum, Lincoln-Douglas, Policy, Congress, or Parliamentary. You pick your side and resolution, then argue out loud against an AI opponent that follows the conventions of whatever format you chose. After the round, you get a judge ballot that breaks down your performance. The AI adapts to the structure of each format, so if you're prepping for a tournament in LD, you practice LD rules; if you're drilling Policy, you get Policy norms. This is the main training page. Pick your format, start speaking, and see how your arguments hold up under scrutiny. When you're done, check the ballot and run another round, or head to the leaderboard to see where you stand against other debaters.

ambassadors.title: Founding Ambassadors · Debatable
ambassadors.script: This is where Debatable is building its first team of student debate coaches. If you are a debater who wants to start or grow a program at your school, you apply here to become a Founding Ambassador. The role is real: you get the title, a reference letter, and metrics you can cite later. In exchange, you run one judged practice each week, bring your own team onto the platform, help launch debate at one nearby school, and host online events like scrimmages or novice round robins. We train you to do all of it: how to recruit, run a first meeting, judge rounds, onboard coaches, and use tools like Tabroom alongside Debatable. The commitment runs from September through April, and ambassadors who succeed often become the founding coaches of the programs they started. There are thirty spots in this first class. To apply, you fill out five required fields and answer one short question: which room you would start, and why you are the person to run it. If you are not a student, the coach track is at /schools.

tournaments.title: Tournaments · Debatable
tournaments.script: You are looking at the tournaments page, where you can enter real brackets that run all the way to a judged final. Every tournament starts with AI prelims that seed the field by win record and speaker points, so you don't wait on byes. The top seeds advance to live video elimination rounds against other debaters, and the AI judge writes a full ballot for every round you debate, including the final. Seven formats are supported: Asian Parliamentary, WSDC, British Parliamentary, APDA, PF, LD, and Policy. Each one keeps its own speech order, timing, and evidence rules the whole way through. Registration is open right now. You can grab a slot, pick the side you want to be tested on, and hold spots in more than one bracket at a time. Entry is free during beta, and the prize is a spot on the leaderboard plus a verifiable credential for a deep run. You do not need a partner; team formats pair you inside the tournament. If no bracket is open for your format, you can spar a human or run a voice round while the next one fills.

online-debate-camp.title: Online Debate Camp · Free AI Sparring & Training · itsdebatable.com
online-debate-camp.script: This is an online debate camp that runs every night of the year. You pick a format, speak your case out loud, the AI opponent responds in real time, and you get a judge ballot at the end. Fifteen formats covered, each one judged by its native paradigm: APDA, British Parliamentary, Worlds, Lincoln-Douglas, Policy, Public Forum, and nine others. Six different AI brains to spar against, each with a distinct argumentative style. Every round is timed correctly, the AI takes points of information mid-speech, and the transcript saves so a coach can review it later. Summer camps cost two to five thousand dollars for two weeks in July. This runs the other fifty weeks. Free during the public beta. No cohort schedule, no travel, no waitlist. You can run a full round in fifteen minutes between classes from a phone or a Chromebook. Use it for pre-tournament prep, post-tournament debrief, or skill-specific drills when you cannot find a human partner. The bottleneck for most debaters is rep volume, not coaching quality. This solves the opponent problem. Start a voice round from the home page, or see a typed round first if you want to read before you speak.

debate-an-ai.title: Debate an AI · Free AI Debate Opponent, Voice or Typed · itsdebatable.com
debate-an-ai.script: This page is where you actually open a round against the AI and start arguing. You can debate out loud in real time or type your speeches asynchronously. Pick a motion from ten competitive formats, take a side, and the AI argues back on a real clock. When the round ends, you get a judge ballot with speaker points and one drill to work on next time. The voice mode runs over WebRTC with sub-300-millisecond latency on a decent connection. The AI listens, interrupts when the warrant is weak, takes points of information in formats that use them, and writes the ballot when time expires. The typed mode lets you drill a specific speech or prep a case without a mic. You can choose which brain runs the round: Claude, GPT, Gemini, Grok, DeepSeek, or Open Lab. Every round runs on a format-aware system prompt that includes speech timing, citation rules, register, judging criteria, and known failure modes for that format. The AI is pinned to one side and defends it. It does not hedge mid-round. The judging panel fans the same round to three models in parallel so the verdict does not lean on a single house style. Start a three-minute round to see how it feels, or open the typed mode if you want to work at your own pace.

debate-online.title: Debate Online Free · With Real People or AI · Debatable
debate-online.script: This is the main portal for all the ways you can debate on Debatable. Three paths, same structure: argue in real time, get a judge ballot. First, you can hit Find and get paired with another debater in under a minute, no wait list, no format requirement. Second, you can open a round against the AI right now, typed or voice, and it will take the other side, field points of information, and score you when the round ends. Third, you can schedule a tournament round with another person on video and let the AI judge write the ballot. All three paths work for ten formats: Asian Parliamentary, WSDC, British Parliamentary, APDA, Public Forum, Lincoln-Douglas, Policy, Congressional Debate, Model UN, and a casual Quick Clash mode for first-timers. The AI knows the speech order, timing, citation rules, and judging criteria for each one. Anonymous visitors get five AI rounds before the soft cap; signing in with Google raises that to ten. The human pairing and scheduled rooms work as a guest too. Everything is free while the site is in beta.

debate-strangers.title: Debate Strangers Online · The Omegle of Debate · Debatable
debate-strangers.script: You're about to be matched with a random debater on the other side of the planet for a live video round. Hit Find, wait about a minute when people are online, and you're face-to-face with a stranger who just did the same thing. A motion lands on the table, you each take a side, and an AI judge writes the ballot when it's over. The point is not to filter for someone you already agree with; the point is to argue with someone who's never heard your case and won't let a weak claim slide just because they like you. Your camera and mic stay off until you accept the match. Either side can end the round whenever they want, and every session has report and block built in. If the queue is empty, you keep your spot while you browse or prep. You can post an open invite for someone to message you directly, or jump to a clearly labeled AI opponent that never sneaks in unannounced. The whole thing runs in your browser.

online-debate-platforms.title: The 7 Best Online Debate Platforms in 2026 · Live, Text & Tournament · Debatable
online-debate-platforms.script: This is a comparison guide to the seven most useful platforms for debating online in twenty twenty-six. Each one does a different job well. Debatable, which is our platform, pairs you with a random stranger on video in under a minute and gives you an AI-written ballot at the end of the round. Mixidea handles team parliamentary rounds for clubs and events. VersyTalks is a text-based community aimed at students. Ziggy Online Debate runs scheduled tournament competitions over an entire season for about thirty to thirty-five dollars. Kialo is an argument-mapping tool, not a live debate room, and it helps you see both sides of an issue before you argue it. DebateArt offers formal written one-on-one debates with community voting and win-loss records. Reddit's Change My View lets you post a single belief and award deltas to anyone who shifts your thinking. The guide walks through what each platform costs, how you find an opponent, and how the verdict works. A table at the top lays out the differences side by side. If you want a live round right now, Debatable and Mixidea are the video options. If you think better in writing, the text platforms give you more time. The page is honest about trade-offs: matchmaking is only as fast as the number of people online.

ai-vs-ai-debate.title: AI vs AI Debate · Watch Two AI Brains Argue · Debatable
ai-vs-ai-debate.script: You pick two AI models, give them a debate topic, and watch them argue a full competitive round against each other. One model takes PROP, the other OPP. They follow real tournament format: timed speeches, points of information at the right windows, extensions where required. At the end, an AI judge writes a ballot with winner, margin, and the central clash. The page lets you choose from six models and fifteen matchups. Claude builds deep case structure; GPT closes hard in rebuttal; DeepSeek runs technical discipline; Grok does sharp cross-examination. The matchup changes how the round plays out on the same motion. You can run Asian Parliamentary, British Parliamentary, World Schools, Public Forum, Lincoln-Douglas, or Quick Clash. Most rounds finish in under two minutes. The tool is useful for prepping a side you rarely get assigned, stress-testing your own case construction, or reviewing tournament motions before competition. Each brain is adversarial by default and won't hedge mid-round. The system learns overnight from top-rated past rounds. Anonymous visitors get five rounds; signing in with Google gets you ten.

debatable.title: Debatable: AI Debate Practice and Voice Sparring
debatable.script: You're on the about page for Debatable, the voice-first debate trainer where you argue out loud against an AI and get a real judge ballot. Pick a motion, choose your side, and speak your case under a clock. The AI pushes back in the format you selected: Policy spreads cards, British Parliamentary runs whip extensions, APDA stays impromptu. When time expires, you get speaker points, the central clash, a winner, and a reason for decision. The opponent runs on six different models depending on the task. Voice rounds use OpenAI Realtime with under two hundred milliseconds of latency, so the AI can interrupt you and take points of information without the usual chatbot lag. Every round you run feeds a nightly distillation process that updates the system prompt with patterns that actually work. The AI you face this week is different from the AI on the same motion last month. Debatable covers ten formats, including World Schools, Lincoln-Douglas, Model UN, and a Quick Clash mode for unstructured warmups. It speaks fourteen languages. The builder is a national APDA champion. The product is free during beta. You can start a voice round, read the founding story, or browse the research corpus from here.

compare-index.title: Debatable Comparisons · How Debatable Compares to General AI Tools · Debatable
compare-index.script: This is the comparison page. It explains how Debatable differs from general AI tools like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini. The core distinction is purpose. General assistants are built for cooperative dialogue: research, drafting, summarizing. Debatable is built for adversarial cognition. It runs live timed rounds in ten competitive formats, from APDA to British Parliamentary to Public Forum. You argue out loud against an AI opponent that can be interrupted, and you get a written judge ballot at the end. The page links to detailed comparisons with ChatGPT and Claude, plus a category guide that maps the broader landscape of AI debate tools. It also explains that Debatable uses six AI models under the hood, including Claude and GPT, but wraps them in format-specific debate structure. Same underlying intelligence in some cases, very different product. The framing here is honest: if you need to research a topic or draft a case broadly, a general assistant is the right pick. If you want to sit in the PM seat at two in the morning and grind a real round before a tournament, that is what this platform is for. Pick the tool that matches the job.

compare-chatgpt.title: Debatable vs ChatGPT for Debate Practice · A Category Comparison · Debatable
compare-chatgpt.script: This page compares two tools you might use for debate practice, but they are not actually in the same category. ChatGPT is a general-purpose assistant built for cooperative help across almost any task. Debatable is a voice-first platform built specifically for competitive rounds. You pick a format, APDA, BP, Public Forum, Lincoln-Douglas, any of fifteen formats, and argue out loud against an opponent that argues back, takes points of information, and writes a judge ballot at the end. The same underlying models sometimes run under the hood; the difference is the wrapper. Debatable prepends format rules, exemplar rounds, and learned patterns from the circuit before your prompt ever reaches the model. It enforces speech order, runs a real timer, lets you interrupt mid-speech, and writes a ballot in the format's actual register. ChatGPT is excellent at research, drafting, and summarization. Debatable is built for the parts you drill: rebuttals under pressure, timed speeches, and ballots that read like a real judge wrote them. Most people should use both. Use ChatGPT for the research. Use Debatable for the round.

compare-claude.title: Debatable vs Claude for Debate Practice · Side-by-Side · Debatable
compare-claude.script: Claude is one of the six reasoning models Debatable runs under the hood. This page walks through what you get when you use Claude directly versus inside Debatable, and when each makes sense. Claude is built for general reasoning across any task. It handles long context well, writes carefully, and excels at brainstorming cases or summarizing research. It is not pinned to a debate format and does not enforce structure by default. When you pick Claude as your brain inside Debatable, the same model runs with four things prepended to every call: a format voice block with speech order and time limits, exemplar past rounds matching your motion and side, learned patterns distilled from top-rated rounds in the past thirty days, and a style fingerprint of how you specifically argue. You also get a live voice round with a real clock, adversarial pushback by default, and a format-pinned judge ballot afterward. Debatable is the only mainstream debate platform that lets you bring your own Anthropic API key. Post-beta that is one dollar per month. You pay Anthropic for the tokens directly, and your round cap is removed. For tasks that are not a round, reach for Claude directly. Long-form research, summarizing a brief, drafting a tournament reflection. Those are what Claude handles excellently out of the box, and the format wrapping would just be friction.

compare-best.title: Best AI Tools for Debate Practice in 2026 · Honest Category Guide · Debatable
compare-best.script: You're looking at a category guide that explains which AI tools actually work for different parts of competitive debate. Competitive debate is not one job. It's research, case construction, drilling speeches, and live sparring, and no single AI covers all of them well. This page walks you through four genres. For background research on a motion, reach for search-grounded tools like Perplexity or Gemini, because you want real citations you can pull up. For drafting cases and stress-testing warrants, use a general-purpose model like Claude or ChatGPT. For collaborative argument mapping with your team, Kialo Edu is the structured platform. For live format-aware sparring with timed speeches and judge ballots, that's what Debatable does. The page explains why format awareness matters: a generic assistant doesn't know the difference between a Public Forum summary and a British Parliamentary extension, so it collapses everything into thoughtful prose. Debatable encodes per-format rules before the model ever sees your message. Most serious debaters use several tools in combination. This guide helps you pick the right one for the job. From here you can start a round, compare tools, or read format guides for the circuit you compete in.

topics-index.title: Debate Topics · PF, LD, Policy, BQ, Worlds · Debatable
topics-index.script: This page is your library of debate topics across every major format. You can see the current resolution for Public Forum, Lincoln-Douglas, Policy, Big Questions, and motion banks for World Schools, British Parliamentary, Asian Parli, Congress, Model UN, and APDA. Each topic links to a guide with framing, common arguments, and a direct path into the AI trainer so you can argue that exact motion out loud right now. Public Forum rotates monthly, LD every two months, Policy runs one topic all season. World Schools mixes prepared motions with impromptu ones released hours before the round. BP and Asian Parli pull from tournament motion banks like WUDC and IIDC. The AI already knows your format's conventions: speech times, judging standards, structure, terminology. It doesn't summarize arguments; it makes them. It baits you, drops weak points, weighs the round, and writes a judge ballot when you finish. If you want to drill a specific resolution or just see what's live right now, this is where you start. From here you can pick a topic and jump straight into a timed round.

topics-apda.title: APDA Debate · American Parliamentary Format · Debatable
topics-apda.script: You're on the APDA page. APDA is American Parliamentary, the main college parliamentary circuit in the United States. Two teams of two. Six speeches, fifteen minutes of prep before the round, no internet, no evidence cards. Government chooses one motion off a three-motion slate and defines it however they want; Opposition responds. The game is being well-read enough that you don't need to look anything up. This page walks through the format: speech times, speaker roles, what points of information are and when you can take them. It explains case archetypes like tight cases and policy cases, and it includes vocabulary the AI uses naturally when you debate it: squirrel, knife, the PM's world. There's a list of major tournaments, sample national-final motions from the last few years, and links to recorded rounds so you can watch real APDA debates on YouTube instead of just reading rules. Scroll down to practice. You pick your seat, the AI plays the other three, and you run the full six-speech round with impromptu prep. Or you can drill specific moves like the Prime Minister Rebuttal or Opposition case structure. The format runs the way it does at Princeton or Yale: no fabricated evidence, real APDA vocabulary, collapse structure in the rebuttals.

topics-asian-parliamentary.title: Asian Parliamentary Debate · Format & Motions · Debatable
topics-asian-parliamentary.script: You're looking at the Asian Parliamentary page, the format that runs nearly every university debate tournament in India. Three speakers per side. Seven-minute substantive speeches, then a four-minute reply from either the first or second speaker. Opposition delivers reply first, Government goes last. This is the structure you'll see at UADC, IIDC, NSDC, and almost every Indian college invitational. The page walks through speaker roles: the Prime Minister defines the motion and opens the case; the Leader of Opposition contests or accepts that definition and builds the counter-case; deputies refute and extend; whips weigh without new matter. It covers points of information, which run from the first to sixth minute of every substantive. There's a section on how Asians differs from British Parliamentary, World Schools, and American formats, plus the prep rules: thirty minutes, no phones, no internet, printed material only. You'll find common motion areas for the Indian circuit, past final motions from major tournaments, and a short FAQ. At the bottom there are practice links where you can spar against an AI in any seat, Prime Minister through Reply.

topics-british-parliamentary.title: British Parliamentary Debate · Format & Roles · Debatable
topics-british-parliamentary.script: This is where you learn British Parliamentary debate, the format that runs at Worlds and most major university tournaments. Four teams of two speakers fight for a ranking against each other, not just a win-loss. Opening Government, Opening Opposition, Closing Government, Closing Opposition. Seven-minute speeches, fifteen minutes of prep, judges rank you first through fourth. If you're heading to WUDC, EUDC, or the Indian circuit tournaments like AIDC or the IIT-Delhi Open, this is the format. The page covers all eight speaker roles in order, from Prime Minister through Opposition Whip, and explains the single biggest tactical move in BP: the extension. That's the new layer or argument that Closing teams have to bring to beat their own Opening bench. Without it, you place fourth. There's also a rundown of how BP differs from Asian Parliamentary, what makes a clean whip speech, how POIs work across four teams, and sample motions from recent finals. If you want to drill individual roles or run full rounds against an AI that plays the other seven seats, there's a practice link at the bottom.

topics-world-schools.title: World Schools Debate · WSDC Motions · Debatable
topics-world-schools.script: World Schools is the three-on-three format used at international high school championships. Each side fields three speakers giving eight-minute substantives, then a four-minute reply from either the first or second speaker. Half the motions are prepared weeks in advance; the other half are impromptu, released about an hour before the round. This page collects recent prepared motions from WSDC 2025, past final motions, and common impromptu themes like international relations, economic justice, and tech ethics. You'll find strategic breakdowns for each speech role: first speakers set the framework, second speakers develop practical analysis, third speakers refute and weigh. The reply speech functions as a biased adjudication, selecting two or three key issues and framing them in your favor. The FAQ at the bottom covers mechanics: how WSDC differs from Asian Parli, how points of information work, which countries compete. There are also short guides on specific moves, like how to structure a reply or open for the proposition. You can practice any WSDC motion against the AI, taking any seat you want, or run a live round with another debater. The AI handles the rest.

topics-lincoln-douglas.title: Lincoln-Douglas Debate · Current LD Resolution · Debatable
topics-lincoln-douglas.script: This is the Lincoln-Douglas topic page. Lincoln-Douglas is a one-on-one values debate where you defend or attack a resolution by establishing a value framework, the ethical concept that matters most, paired with a criterion that measures whether that value is upheld. Most rounds are won at the framework layer before the contention debate even matters. The current two-month resolution asks whether a just society should prioritize restorative justice over retributive justice in the criminal legal system. On this page you can review common Aff and Neg frameworks for the current topic, browse past resolutions going back a year, and read explanations of what makes LD structurally different from Policy or Public Forum. The FAQ covers value-criterion mechanics, speech times, and how kritiks and theory work in circuit LD versus traditional LD. When you're ready to drill the current topic, click the practice link and you'll get matched against an AI opponent who runs framework debate, contentions, and cross-examination, then gives you a full ballot.

topics-public-forum.title: Public Forum Debate · Current Resolution · Debatable
topics-public-forum.script: This is the Public Forum debate hub. Public Forum is the NSDA's two-on-two high-school format, where the resolution rotates every month and you face lay judges who expect plain English. Speeches are short: four minutes constructive, three rebuttal, three summary, two final focus. No spreading, no kritiks, no theory. You win with clean evidence and comparative weighing. The current resolution is whether the benefits of US federal government expansion of artificial intelligence regulation outweigh the harms. The page walks through Pro and Con framing, then lists the last six months of topics. Each topic has a practice button that loads it into a live AI debate round. Pro typically argues bias reduction, misinformation control, labor transition support, and national security. Weighing tends to be probability times scope: small harms across millions of users. Con argues innovation chill, geopolitical loss, open-source kill, and regulatory capture. Weighing tends to be magnitude and irreversibility: once you lose a research generation or open ecosystem, you don't get it back. Below the resolution framing you'll find a FAQ covering speech lengths, evidence standards, crossfire mechanics, final focus structure, and how PF differs from Lincoln-Dash-Douglas. At the bottom, links to practice rounds and other debate formats.

topics-policy.title: Policy Debate · Current CX Resolution & Cases · Debatable
topics-policy.script: This page is your control center for Policy debate, also called Cross-Examination or CX. Policy runs one resolution for the entire season, September through June. The current high-school topic asks whether the federal government should substantially increase investment in domestic semiconductor manufacturing. That means affirmatives run plans to expand CHIPS Act funding, build federally owned fabs, or fund workforce training. Negatives attack with disadvantages like China escalation, counterplans like a states alternative, kritiks targeting capitalism or securitization, and topicality arguments parsing the word substantially. The page walks you through the off-case toolkit: what a DA is, how a counterplan works, how kritiks flip the framework. You'll also see the common affirmative case areas, answers to the biggest negatives, and the last five annual resolutions. Below that sits a full FAQ explaining Policy's structure: eight speeches across ninety minutes, spreading at three hundred fifty words per minute, and the philosophical split between policy and kritik debate. At the bottom you can practice the semiconductor topic against an AI opponent that runs theory, disadvantages, and line-by-line rebuttals at circuit speed.

topics-congress.title: Student Congress Debate · Bills & Speeches · Debatable
topics-congress.script: This is the Student Congress page. Congress is a mock legislature format where you deliver three-minute speeches supporting or opposing bills from a tournament docket, then face two minutes of questions from the chamber. You're scored individually against every other speaker in the room. The most important speech is authorship. That's the first affirmative on a bill, usually given by whoever wrote it. Authorship sets the framing for the entire chamber's debate, and strong authors get ranked highly by every judge before anyone moves on. A good authorship hits four beats: hook, problem, mechanism, impact. Three minutes total. After authorship come constructive speeches, alternating affirmative and negative. Repetition gets penalized; clash gets rewarded. Late in debate on a bill, you can give a crystallization speech that synthesizes the round and tells the parliamentarian who wins and why. The page walks through parliamentary procedure shortcuts, common docket themes like healthcare and tech regulation, and explains how scoring works. Judges rank your speeches one to eight. Your best three or four rounds count toward elimination. You can practice authorship, workshop amendments, or drill questions with the AI. It plays the parliamentarian and gives ranking-style feedback. Start a practice round or keep browsing the other debate formats.

topics-mun.title: Model UN · Format, Procedure & Practice · Debatable
topics-mun.script: Model UN teaches you to negotiate like a country. You're assigned a UN member state, dropped into a simulated committee with fifteen to two hundred other delegates, and given a docket of global policy questions. Your job is to form blocs with countries that share your interests, draft working papers that consolidate into resolutions, and speak just enough from the floor to get your name on the ballot without hogging time better spent writing operative clauses. The format rewards position-paper accuracy, parliamentary procedure, and amendment authorship as much as the speeches you give. Sessions cycle between speakers' list, moderated caucus for structured mini-speeches, and unmoderated caucus where the real negotiation happens off mic. You'll write preambulatory clauses that justify and operative clauses that command, choosing verbs by strength: softer language builds General Assembly consensus faster; stronger verbs survive Security Council amendment battles. Crisis committees add real-time evolving events and individual directives. The AI here plays the opposing bloc so you can stress-test your country's positions, workshop your opening speech, or run a moderated exchange before the conference. Practice a delegate speech to get started.

topics-big-questions.title: Big Questions Debate · Current Resolution · Debatable
topics-big-questions.script: This is the Big Questions guide. Big Questions is NSDA's philosophy format where you debate the same resolution all year, arguing both sides across a tournament. This year's topic asks whether consciousness can be fully explained by physical processes. On the affirmative, you defend physicalism: mental states reduce to brain states, functional patterns, or representations of representations. Philosophers like Dennett, Putnam, and the Churchlands back this side. On the negative, you argue phenomenal experience resists physical explanation. You work with Chalmers' hard problem, Jackson's Mary's Room thought experiment, and property dualism. The format keeps speeches short, five minutes down to three, and bans circuit tactics like kritiks or theory. You need to make the philosophy accessible to lay judges, which means no jargon and no spreading. Past topics covered free will, moral realism, and whether self-interest drives human behavior. The page lists common frameworks for both sides and walks through the speech structure. You can practice the current resolution here against an AI opponent that argues either physicalism or its negation, whichever side you need to test.

judge.title: AI Debate Judge · Get a real ballot on any round · Debatable
judge.script: This page gives you a debate judge in software. Paste the full transcript of a round you already debated, both sides, every speech you have. Tell it the motion and the format. The AI reads the whole flow, identifies the key clashes, and returns a complete ballot: who won, why, and speaker points for each speaker. The reason-for-decision names the arguments that decided the round and flags what got dropped, the way a flowing judge would write it. It was built for the lay-judge gap. Most Public Forum rounds are decided by parent judges who never flowed. You can give a clean summary and still lose the ballot because the judge voted on confidence instead of weighing. This gives you an objective second opinion that engages the arguments. It judges through your format's real adjudication norms, not generic feedback. PF gets summary-to-final-focus rules. LD gets framework clash. Policy gets collapse. You judge the round yourself first, then reveal what the AI called. The more of the round you paste, the sharper the ballot. It works for PF, LD, Policy, Congress, APDA, BP, Worlds, Asian Parli, and a quick-clash mode. Treat it as a second read, not a replacement for a real panel.

team.title: The team · Debatable
team.script: This is the team page. Four people are building Debatable, and all of them debate. The founder is a national APDA champion who built the entire product from scratch. He started the company solo in March and they now ship updates most days, sometimes more than once. The engineer comes from Meta and Nvidia and runs infrastructure and cost control. The debate lead captained Zimbabwe at Worlds, coaches Ethiopia's national team, and owns format accuracy and judge quality. The fourth person runs growth and marketing. The company is also hiring thirty paid ambassadors for the coming debate season. These are debaters and coaches who will bring the product onto their own circuits. If you compete somewhere Debatable hasn't reached yet, you can apply to become one of the founding ambassadors. Every person on this team either competed at a high level or built products at scale, and often both.

early.title: Founding cohort · Debatable
early.script: This is the founding cohort signup for Debatable's first online debate events. You're joining the invite list for scheduled rounds against real people, judged by AI, scored on the same twenty-five to thirty scale your certificate uses. Founding cohort members get first invites before public scheduling opens, matchmaking in your chosen format at your skill level, and direct input on how the events get organized. The signup takes twenty seconds: email, optional name, your skill level from novice to champion, your preferred format like APDA or British Parliamentary, and your time zone. No account, no password, no app. Once you're on the list, we email you when the first round opens. You can take a voice round in the meantime so you have a credential ready when invites go out. The list is low volume and only covers scheduled events, product updates, and application openings. If you want to help pilot the format and get early access to live competition, this is the one signup that puts you there.

twitch-plugin.title: Twitch AI Judge · Room Judge · Debatable
twitch-plugin.script: This page explains how to use the AI judge as a Twitch extension. You can drop it into a panel, overlay, or dashboard tab so viewers see the ballot build in real time while watching a debate stream. Start by typing the channel name and pressing Open. A compact judge console appears. Hit Start listening, then share the tab playing the stream and tick the box to include audio. The AI listens through your browser, fills in the flow as people speak, and pulls in public chat as audience signal. Chat does not decide the ballot. It shows the judge where viewers are confused or persuaded, but the decision still comes from argument clash and warranted claims. You can ask for a live read at any point or wait until the end to press End and write ballot. The extension surface and chat listener are live now. A full Twitch Extension can point at the compact URL directly. Broadcasters can run this from a dashboard tab and route audio in without voice guidance bleeding into the stream unless they want it to.

zoom-plugin.title: Zoom AI Judge · Room Judge · Debatable
zoom-plugin.script: You can run the AI judge inside your Zoom meeting right now. Open the judge in a browser window beside Zoom, then tell your browser to capture Zoom's window audio or use your microphone if the meeting plays through speakers. The judge listens live and writes a ballot when you end the session. This works today as a side-by-side setup. There's also a compact view you can use while setting up a native Zoom app, which will eventually pull audio directly from Zoom's server once the marketplace approves the required permissions. That server path skips the browser and grabs the meeting stream, video, and transcript in real time. For now, the sidecar is fully functional. One ground rule: the host should tell everyone Debatable is listening before the round begins, and the judge will stop if participants sound caught off guard or push back. Open the judge to get started.

counter.title: Counter: counter your draft, or defend it out loud · Debatable
counter.script: Counter reads what you wrote and finds the crack before it matters. Highlight any paragraph from your essay, thesis, or pitch, and the AI names the weakest claim, stacks three rebuttals under it, and hands you the question your reader asks first. Then you can defend it out loud: same passage, voice mode, one precise question at a time, the way a real viva or panel actually runs. Pick who's across the table, admissions officer, thesis committee, opposing counsel, editor, VC, and Counter presses where that reader presses. The tool is a Chrome extension, still in beta, so you sideload the dev build instead of installing from the store. Works on any webpage, in PDFs, and inside Google Docs. The free tier gives you ten drills total; the individual plan is ten dollars a year for two hundred fifty drills a month and HD voice. Everything is free while the product is in beta. The AI speaks Hindi, Marathi, Tamil, Telugu, and a dozen other languages if you need a regional-medium viva. Download the build, flip on developer mode in Chrome, load the unpacked folder, and start drilling on the argument you weren't ready to defend.

oral-exam-prep.title: Viva Practice Online · AI Oral Exam Prep · Debatable
oral-exam-prep.script: This room is for oral exam practice. If you have a thesis defense, a PhD viva, or any exam where you have to answer out loud while someone interrupts, you defend your argument here against an AI examiner. You make a claim about your research, the examiner finds the soft spot, and you have to hold your reasoning together across follow-ups. The session is voice, not text. You speak, it listens, it challenges, you respond. The examiner works through six standard pressure points: your summary, your methodology, your limitations, your contribution to the field, what you would do differently, and how your work stands up when a core assumption gets removed. After each answer it follows up on the weakest part instead of moving to the next question. You get scored on clarity, reasoning, responsiveness, listening, and structure. Same scoring system Debatable uses everywhere else, so your progress shows up on one communication profile across debate rounds, oral exams, and interview prep. The session takes a few minutes and you can practice in dozens of languages. Run it once a day for the week before your real exam, on a different section each time, until defending any chapter out loud feels like reflex.

benchmark.title: Can AI Judge a Debate Round? The Debatable Judge Benchmark · Debatable
benchmark.script: Debatable trains you by arguing against AI and getting judge ballots. This page exists to answer whether AI can judge a debate well enough to matter. Two tracks. Track one is a live leaderboard of which AI lab's model judges best, tested on real British Parliamentary tournament rounds from circuits like Yale IV, Hart House, and WUDC opens. An experienced human chair sat each round and made the call. Each model gets the same judge flow, ranks all four teams, and we measure how often it agrees with the human. OpenAI leads at seventy-nine percent agreement; the random coin-flip baseline is fifty. Small sample, so single-digit gaps are noise. Track two is harder and emptier: platform rounds where an expert rates the full transcript, then we score the AI ballot against that rating. Right now that set is zero rounds. The page reports only what reconciles to a live database, and the expert-rated corpus is being built. You can test the judge yourself by pasting any round transcript and getting a full ballot back. If you're an AI lab and think your model judges better, we will run it and publish the result.

atlas.title: The Debate Atlas · Debatable
atlas.script: This is the Debate Atlas, an internal tool that maps every serious debate program in the world. You can filter by format and level, see how many programs exist in each region, and track which ones are in conversation, partnered, or still prospects. The map shows around 200 competitive circuit schools in the United States sitting on top of 24,000 high schools with no debate program at all. That gap is the market. You can toggle overlays to see tournament locations, time zone bands, and density heat maps that show where debate clusters. The revenue tab models what scaling into India, the UK, Southeast Asia, and other circuits could look like over the next two years, with conservative and bullish scenarios based on school counts and local pricing. If you run a program or debate yourself, you can add a pin. The pipeline stages are editable so you can mark which schools you have actually partnered with before exporting a screenshot. This is a working strategy tool, not a public directory.

live.title: Debatable · Live debates with humans on your motion
live.script: This is where you debate another person live, with an AI writing the judge ballot. You pick a motion and format, choose when you want to debate, then post it to the board or accept someone else's open slot. Both of you join the same video room when the round starts. The format timer runs through each speech automatically. One person talks and transcribes while the other listens and preps. After the final speech, the AI delivers a tournament-style ballot with winner, speaker points, a reason for decision, and per-speaker feedback. The result saves to both debaters' records. You can schedule a round days out and get a calendar invite, or click spar now for an immediate random partner. The system handles the video link, the clock, and the judging, so you just show up and debate. Every format from APDA to Policy is supported. You'll need a laptop with a camera and microphone, a Google account to sign in, and Chrome or Safari if you want live mic transcription during speeches. Rounds take ten minutes for Quick Clash, up to ninety for Policy. Right now the board shows how many people are looking for partners, how many rounds are scheduled, and which slots are still open.

spar.title: Spar · Debate it live with another debater · Debatable
spar.script: This is where you debate a real person, not the AI. Choose your format and optionally add a judge-paradigm note for your opponent to see. If they also wrote one, you'll both review and accept before the round begins. Hit the queue and you'll be matched within about a minute. If nobody shows up immediately, you can practise against the AI while you wait without losing your place in line. There's also an open waitlist where you can post your availability, browse other debaters looking for rounds, and message them directly to schedule something later. After the round, an AI judge writes your ballot. You need to sign in to post to the waitlist or send messages, but you can browse and queue as a guest.

leaderboard.title: Leaderboard · Debatable
leaderboard.script: This is where you see who's climbing the ranks on voice rounds judged by AI. Two separate leaderboards run here. One tracks live voice debates between humans. The other ranks solo speakers based purely on their speaking score. Both use the same AI ballot system, so a solo practice round gets judged exactly like a head-to-head match. Only voice rounds count. If you type your arguments instead, they save to your history but never touch the leaderboard. The page shows you what's happening right now: how many rounds ran today, who's speaking live at this moment, and the current top score. Every number on the board comes from the AI judge's written ballot, scored on the standard fifty to one hundred speaker point scale. Six different models rotate as judge, Claude, GPT, Gemini, Grok, DeepSeek, and Open Lab, but they all follow the same rubric. Sign in once and your voice rounds start counting automatically. You can jump into a solo voice round to practice or join an open room to debate someone live.

livedebates.title: Live Debates · Debate real people online, with an AI judge
livedebates.script: This is where you debate real people online and get an instant judge ballot. You join a queue, get matched with a debater anywhere in the world, argue a timed round, and an AI judge writes the full decision the moment you finish. You can pick a format first. Quick Clash for a fast round, or a full competitive format like APDA, British Parliamentary, World Schools, Policy, and several others. Each one runs on the real speech order, clock, and evidence rules, not a generic chat. When you join the queue, you get paired with someone else who is online and ready. If no one is available, you can fall back to an AI opponent. The round is timed. You speak against the clock, take points of information if the format allows, and think on your feet. When time runs out, the AI judge weighs the round on the flow and writes a reason for decision immediately. If you want to organize a round on your own time, there is a board where you can post that you are looking, browse who else is open, and message a debater directly. The site was built by a national APDA champion, so every format reflects how it is actually judged in competition.

float.title: Debate in the background · Float a round over your apps · itsdebatable.com
float.script: You can keep a live debate running while you use other apps. Start a voice round on the site, then either tap Float over apps or turn on auto-float to pop the round into a small window that stays visible no matter where you go. The mic and AI keep working. You can open Slack, take notes, or browse anything while the debate continues in the corner. The floating window shows a live transcript of the latest line, the current speech timer, and one-tap controls to cut in with a point of information, mute yourself, or end the round. On Chrome and Edge, the window actually floats over your desktop apps, even when the browser is minimized. On Firefox and Safari, it docks to a corner of the site instead, but audio still runs in the background. There's also an optional launcher extension you can download and install manually. It puts a toolbar button in Chrome or Edge that opens a new debate from any tab. You don't need it to float rounds, but it's there if you want a shortcut. The whole thing runs in your browser with no separate install.

floor.title: The Floor · Debatable
floor.script: You're looking at The Floor, a design preview for a feature that doesn't work yet. This is where you would watch debate rounds and predict the winner before the verdict posts. Right now, everything you see is seeded data: AI personas debating each other, outcomes generated by a rating model, and matchups that aren't tied to real ballots. The interface is built and running, but you can't make calls, credits don't move, and nothing here has any cash value. What's working: the ledger tracks balances, the board updates, and settlement logic is in place. What's missing: real rounds with actual transcripts, server-side speech storage, and the age gate that would need to exist before this opens. Once it does, you'll read the matchup, pick the debater you think the AI judge will side with, and see how your read compares to the crowd. Each round shows its format and judge lens up front, so you know whether the ballot prioritizes weighing, dropped arguments, or framework before you commit. Two verdicts post: the AI judge's winner and the crowd favorite. When they split, that gap is the story. For now, this page just shows you the shape of what's coming.
